---
title: "Local Python Tools"
description: "Install Python and use VS Code, Jupyter, and the terminal as one local learning toolkit."
execute:
  echo: true
---

## Why this matters

Install Python and use VS Code, Jupyter, and the terminal as one local learning toolkit.

This lesson combines related subtopics that belong together in one learning
conversation. You will still pause for a quiz after each section, but you do not
need to jump between separate pages while building one mental model.

::: {.questions}
### Questions

By the end of this lesson, you should be able to answer:

- How do the sections in **Local Python Tools** fit together?
- Which small example demonstrates each section?
- Which debugging clue should I check first for each section?
:::

::: {.learning-objectives}
### Learning objectives

You will practice how to:

- explain the shared concept for this lesson;
- use each section as one step in a larger workflow;
- complete 4 short section quizzes before moving on;
- connect examples, mistakes, and debugging routines.
:::

## Lesson map

- Section 1: **Install Python** — Install Python locally and verify the correct interpreter and package installer are available.
- Section 2: **VS Code** — Use VS Code as a beginner-friendly editor for Python projects.
- Section 3: **Jupyter** — Use Jupyter notebooks for exploration, notes, code, output, and plots.
- Section 4: **Terminal Basics** — Use the terminal to navigate folders, run commands, and start Python scripts.

## Section 1: Install Python

Install Python locally and verify the correct interpreter and package installer are available.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

Installing Python is like putting a translator on your own laptop instead of borrowing
one online.
:::

### Mental model

A local Python installation gives your computer its own interpreter so scripts can run
outside the browser.

### Tiny example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```bash
python --version
python -m pip --version
python -c "print('Python works')"
```

### Walkthrough

1. `python --version` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
2. `python -m pip --version` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
3. `python -c "print('Python works')"` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the tiny example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
Installing multiple Python versions can make commands point to different interpreters.
Always verify with python --version and python -c.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Section checkpoint

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "getting-started-install-python",
  "title": "Install Python checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Install Python?",
      "options": [
        "A local Python installation gives your computer its own interpreter so scripts can run outside the browser.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: A local Python installation gives your computer its own interpreter so scripts can run outside the browser."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: Installing Python is like putting a translator on your own laptop instead of borrowing one online."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the tiny example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Section 2: VS Code

Use VS Code as a beginner-friendly editor for Python projects.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

VS Code is like a tidy study desk with a notebook, calculator, file cabinet, and
magnifying glass in one place.
:::

### Mental model

VS Code helps you edit files, run programs, inspect folders, and debug from one
workspace.

### Tiny example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
print("Save this as hello.py")
print("Run it from VS Code")
```

### Walkthrough

1. `print("Save this as hello.py")` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
2. `print("Run it from VS Code")` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the tiny example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
Opening a single file instead of the project folder can hide imports, terminals, and
settings. Open the folder that contains the project.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Section checkpoint

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "getting-started-vscode",
  "title": "VS Code checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes VS Code?",
      "options": [
        "VS Code helps you edit files, run programs, inspect folders, and debug from one workspace.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: VS Code helps you edit files, run programs, inspect folders, and debug from one workspace."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: VS Code is like a tidy study desk with a notebook, calculator, file cabinet, and magnifying glass in one place."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the tiny example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Section 3: Jupyter

Use Jupyter notebooks for exploration, notes, code, output, and plots.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

A notebook is like a lab journal where each experiment sits next to its observation.
:::

### Mental model

Jupyter notebooks mix explanation and executable code in small cells, which supports
prediction and experimentation.

### Tiny example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
temperature_c = 21
temperature_f = temperature_c * 9 / 5 + 32
temperature_f
```

### Walkthrough

1. `temperature_c = 21` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
2. `temperature_f = temperature_c * 9 / 5 + 32` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
3. `temperature_f` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the tiny example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
A notebook remembers variables from previous cells. If you are unsure about state,
restart the kernel and run all cells top to bottom.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Section checkpoint

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "getting-started-jupyter",
  "title": "Jupyter checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Jupyter?",
      "options": [
        "Jupyter notebooks mix explanation and executable code in small cells, which supports prediction and experimentation.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: Jupyter notebooks mix explanation and executable code in small cells, which supports prediction and experimentation."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: A notebook is like a lab journal where each experiment sits next to its observation."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the tiny example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Section 4: Terminal Basics

Use the terminal to navigate folders, run commands, and start Python scripts.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

The terminal is like the address bar for your computer: you type where to go and what
action to perform.
:::

### Mental model

The terminal is a text interface for asking your operating system to do precise tasks.

### Tiny example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```bash
pwd
ls
python hello.py
```

### Walkthrough

1. `pwd` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
2. `ls` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
3. `python hello.py` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the tiny example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
Running a command from the wrong folder causes file-not-found errors. Check pwd before
assuming the file is missing.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Section checkpoint

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "getting-started-terminal-basics",
  "title": "Terminal Basics checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Terminal Basics?",
      "options": [
        "The terminal is a text interface for asking your operating system to do precise tasks.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: The terminal is a text interface for asking your operating system to do precise tasks."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: The terminal is like the address bar for your computer: you type where to go and what action to perform."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the tiny example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Notebook and Colab practice

Open a blank notebook at <https://colab.new>. Use one section at a time: copy the
tiny example, predict the result, run it, answer the section quiz, and then move
to the next section. This is better than copying the entire page at once.

## Instructor note

<details class="instructor">
<summary>Teaching notes</summary>

- Treat each section as a short teaching episode.
- Pause for the section quiz before introducing the next section.
- Ask learners to compare sections: what stayed the same, and what changed?
- If time is short, teach the first two sections live and assign the rest as practice.

</details>

## Key points

::: {.key-points}
- **Install Python**: A local Python installation gives your computer its own interpreter so scripts can run
outside the browser.
- **VS Code**: VS Code helps you edit files, run programs, inspect folders, and debug from one
workspace.
- **Jupyter**: Jupyter notebooks mix explanation and executable code in small cells, which supports
prediction and experimentation.
- **Terminal Basics**: The terminal is a text interface for asking your operating system to do precise tasks.
- Use the section quizzes as gates: review before moving on if a quiz feels uncertain.
:::

## References

- Python downloads: https://www.python.org/downloads/
- Python Tutorial: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/
- Google Colab overview: https://colab.research.google.com/notebooks/basic_features_overview.ipynb
- Jupyter documentation: https://docs.jupyter.org/
- VS Code Python tutorial: https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/python/python-tutorial
- Quarto OJS documentation: https://quarto.org/docs/interactive/ojs/
- ipywidgets documentation: https://ipywidgets.readthedocs.io/en/stable/
- Command line crash course: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn_web_development/Getting_started/Environment_setup/Command_line
